prior.title: EU CBAM
prior.summary: IN FORCE — full compliance regime operational since 1 January 2026. Transitional phase complete. ACTION NOW: Verify all EU importers of covered goods have authorized declarant status (deadline 31 March 2026). Update customs documentation workflows to capture CBAM data. Train customs brokerage teams on CBAM reporting requirements. Owner: Customs + Compliance.
prior.what_is_it: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) under Regulation (EU) 2023/956 requires EU importers to purchase CBAM certificates reflecting the embedded carbon emissions in imported goods — steel, iron, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity. Fully operational from 1 January 2026 after a transitional reporting phase (2023-2025). Enforced by national competent authorities in each EU Member State. CBAM certificate price tracks the EU ETS allowance price.
prior.why_matters: From 2026, EU importers of covered goods bear the full carbon cost equivalent to what EU producers pay under ETS. For freight forwarders: (1) customs clearance workflows change — only authorized CBAM declarants can import covered goods (>12,000 operators applied by January 2026, >4,100 approved), (2) importers must report embedded emissions in each shipment, (3) forwarders handling steel/aluminium/cement freight need to verify CBAM declarant registration numbers before customs clearance, (4) CBAM certificate sales postponed to 1 February 2027 — price will track EU ETS quarterly average (~€68-81/tCO₂).
key_data: Full application: 1 January 2026 (transitional phase ended 31 December 2025)
Covered goods: steel, iron, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen, electricity
De minimis: shipments >50 tonnes require authorized declarant status
Authorized declarant deadline: 31 March 2026 for existing importers
Certificate sales start: 1 February 2027 (postponed from January 2026)
Certificate price: tracks EU ETS allowance price (~€68-81/tCO₂ Q1 2026)
>12,000 operators applied, >4,100 approved as authorized declarants (January 2026)
10,483 import declarations with CBAM goods validated in first week of 2026
prior.reasoning: CBAM definitive phase NOW — customs documentation and carbon costs on all covered EU imports. Direct operational impact for staging equipment containing steel/aluminium.
prior.full_brief:
# EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)

**Regulation (EU) 2023/956 | Definitive Regime In Force from 1 January 2026**
**Format: Regulatory Fact Document | Jurisdiction: EU | Item ID: 51b2c91e-776f-42f5-a799-a957312d4e56**

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document analyses Regulation (EU) 2023/956 establishing the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), filtered through the operational profile of a freight forwarder active across the Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes, with cargo verticals spanning live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian. Transport mode priority is air primary, road secondary, ocean tertiary.

**Conventions used throughout:**
- "The workspace" refers to the freight forwarder described in the workspace profile. No company or individual name is used.
- "Legal Confirmation Required" flags items requiring qualified legal counsel review.
- Inline citations follow each subsection. Sources are classified by type per the source hierarchy.
- Severity labels appear in space-separated form (e.g., ACTION REQUIRED).

**Document currency:** Reflects the CBAM definitive regime as confirmed live on 1 January 2026. The most recent development referenced in the source content is the publication of the first quarterly CBAM certificate price on 7 April 2026 and the "CBAM Goes Live: Guidelines for Operational Procedures" guidance document published 19 March 2026.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, European Commission DG TAXUD, accessed from source content dated to April 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2 — EU Commission implementation portal).*

---

# What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## The Regulation

CBAM is established under Regulation (EU) 2023/956 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 10 May 2023. Its core mechanism: EU importers of designated carbon-intensive goods must acquire and surrender CBAM certificates corresponding to the embedded greenhouse gas emissions of those goods, ensuring imported goods face a carbon cost equivalent to that borne by EU producers under the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS).

**Covered goods (definitive regime):** cement, iron and steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen — and selected precursors within those categories.

**Threshold:** Importers moving more than 50 tonnes (single mass-based threshold) of CBAM goods into the EU must hold authorised declarant status.

**Certificate price:** Calculated on the basis of the EU ETS allowance auction price. For 2026 Q1, the first certificate price has been published (as of 7 April 2026). The pricing cadence shifts from quarterly average (2026) to weekly average from 2027 onward.

**Carbon price deduction:** If the importer can demonstrate that a carbon price was already paid during production in the country of origin, the corresponding amount may be deducted from the CBAM certificate obligation.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex, 10 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956 (Tier 1 — binding EU law). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

## Why It Applies to the Workspace

The workspace operates as a freight forwarder. Whether and how CBAM directly binds the workspace depends on the role it assumes transaction-by-transaction — specifically, whether it acts as an indirect customs representative for EU importers of CBAM-covered goods.

Under Regulation (EU) 2023/956, the obligation to hold authorised declarant status and to surrender CBAM certificates falls primarily on the **EU importer**. However, the regulation expressly permits EU importers to be represented by their **indirect customs representatives** when applying for and holding authorised declarant status, and indirect customs representatives may themselves apply for that status on behalf of importers.

**Direct applicability to the workspace's cargo verticals:** The workspace's named verticals — live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production assets, high-value automotive, and humanitarian cargo — are **not** in the current CBAM-covered goods list (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, electricity, hydrogen). However:

- **High-value automotive:** Vehicles and automotive parts are not currently in scope, but steel and aluminium components, which are used in automotive manufacturing and may travel as precursor goods in supply chains the workspace serves, are covered. Whether the workspace's clients import steel or aluminium as part of their supply chains — as distinct from importing finished vehicles — is a factual determination the workspace must make for each client.
- **Indirect customs representation:** Where the workspace acts as indirect customs representative for a client importing any CBAM-covered goods (however incidental to the workspace's primary verticals), the workspace may be drawn into the CBAM compliance chain. **Legal Confirmation Required** on whether indirect customs representation for CBAM-covered goods creates joint or several liability for certificate obligations under the workspace's specific Member State of establishment.
- **Freight forwarder role in documentation:** Regardless of direct declarant status, customs clearance workflow changes — CBAM account numbers or application reference numbers must now appear in import documentation for covered goods above the 50-tonne threshold, and the workspace's customs teams must be equipped to handle this.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956 (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### ACTION REQUIRED — Verify indirect customs representation exposure across the client portfolio

**Action:** Audit the client portfolio for any instances where the workspace acts as indirect customs representative for importers of CBAM-covered goods (steel, iron, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen, electricity). Confirm whether any such imports exceed the 50-tonne mass threshold triggering authorised declarant obligations.

**Why now:** The definitive regime is live as of 1 January 2026. Imports of covered goods above the threshold made without a valid CBAM account number or application reference number are non-compliant from that date. The workspace's customs brokerage function cannot clear such goods without this reference.

**Who is affected:** Customs brokerage and operations teams handling EU import clearance, specifically on trade lanes arriving into the EU from the Americas and Asia where CBAM-covered goods are most likely to originate.

**Cost impact:** Direct certificate cost falls on the importer, not the workspace. However, operational exposure — delays, clearance failures, or liability under indirect representation — is a workspace risk. Current certificate rate for 2026 Q1 has been published by the Commission (exact €/tonne figure not reproduced in the source content provided; the workspace should retrieve it directly from the Commission's dedicated certificate price page). **Legal Confirmation Required** on liability scope for indirect customs representatives.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2). Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

---

### ACTION REQUIRED — Update customs documentation workflows to capture CBAM account numbers

**Action:** Modify import documentation templates and customs management systems to include a field for the importer's CBAM account number or application reference number for all shipments potentially containing covered goods. Brief customs operations staff on the requirement.

**Why now:** As of 1 January 2026, EU importers (or their indirect customs representatives) importing more than 50 tonnes of CBAM goods must present a CBAM account number or application reference number. The workspace's clearance workflows must be able to capture and transmit this data to national customs systems, which are now integrated with the CBAM Registry.

**Who is affected:** Customs operations, documentation teams, IT or systems administrators responsible for customs management software.

**Cost impact:** Internal workflow change cost only at the workspace level. External cost (CBAM certificates) falls on the importer client.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "CBAM Goes Live: Guidelines for Operational Procedures for 1 January 2026," published 19 March 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2). Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

---

### MONITORING — Track CBAM scope expansion and forthcoming Commission review

**Action:** Assign responsibility to a designated team member to monitor Commission announcements on potential CBAM scope expansion and the 17 December 2025 Commission proposal to close loopholes and prevent circumvention.

**Why now:** The Commission has already proposed measures to strengthen CBAM efficacy (announced 17 December 2025, per source content) and a call for evidence on emissions methodology was launched 28 August 2025. Future scope expansion could bring additional goods into the regime that are more relevant to the workspace's verticals.

**Who is affected:** Commercial leadership (scope expansion affects client advisory capability) and compliance team.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "Commission strengthens the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism," 17 December 2025. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The workspace's supply-chain role per the workspace profile is **freight forwarder**. Under CBAM, the compliance chain operates as follows:

**Primary obligated party:** The EU importer — the entity established in an EU Member State that is placing CBAM-covered goods into free circulation in the EU. This party must: (1) hold authorised declarant status; (2) purchase CBAM certificates from the national competent authority in the Member State of establishment; (3) declare embedded emissions annually; and (4) surrender the corresponding number of certificates.

**Indirect customs representative:** An EU importer may be represented by an indirect customs representative. The regulation expressly includes indirect customs representatives as entities that may apply for authorised declarant status. Where the workspace functions as an indirect customs representative for an importer of CBAM-covered goods above the 50-tonne threshold, it may sit within the compliance chain.

**Freight forwarder as arranger, not declarant:** In transactions where the workspace does not hold indirect customs representative status — acting instead as a pure transport and logistics arranger — it does not directly bear CBAM declarant obligations, but it remains operationally affected through documentation requirements at the customs interface.

**Role-by-role position (workspace profile applied):**

| Transaction Role | CBAM Compliance Position |
|---|---|
| Freight forwarder only (no customs representation) | Not a declarant; operational documentation exposure only |
| Indirect customs representative for EU importer of covered goods | May be drawn into authorised declarant obligation — **Legal Confirmation Required** |
| Freight forwarder for non-covered goods verticals (live events, fine art, luxury, film, humanitarian) | Not in scope for CBAM at current covered goods list |

**Legal Confirmation Required:** The liability consequences of acting as indirect customs representative in CBAM transactions — specifically, joint and several liability exposure — must be confirmed with legal counsel in each Member State of establishment where the workspace files declarations.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32023R0956 (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

---

# Threshold Questions

The following definitional questions determine whether and how CBAM obligations attach to the workspace's operations. Status noted for each.

**Q1: Does the workspace act as indirect customs representative for any EU importers of CBAM-covered goods?**
Status: **Factual determination required by the workspace.** The answer depends on the workspace's actual client portfolio and the customs representation arrangements in place for each import transaction.

**Q2: Do any such imports exceed the 50-tonne mass-based threshold?**
Status: **Factual determination required by the workspace.** The regulation sets a single mass-based threshold of 50 tonnes per importer per year (precise measurement basis per the regulation and implementing acts). Below this threshold, authorised declarant status is not required.

**Q3: Does the workspace's indirect representation role, where applicable, extend to holding authorised declarant status on the importer's behalf?**
Status: **Legal Confirmation Required.** The regulation permits indirect customs representatives to apply for authorised declarant status, but whether this creates a mandatory requirement or a permissive election, and what liability flows from each path, requires legal review specific to the Member State(s) of establishment.

**Q4: Are any of the workspace's cargo verticals — specifically high-value automotive components — CBAM-covered goods?**
Status: **Factual determination required per shipment.** Finished vehicles are not in scope. Steel and aluminium as materials or components, if imported in their own right above the threshold, are in scope. Whether clients route such materials through the workspace's freight arrangements requires a client-by-client review.

**Q5: Will the CBAM scope expand to cover goods more directly relevant to the workspace's verticals?**
Status: **Monitoring required.** The Commission has proposed anti-circumvention measures and launched a call for evidence on methodology. No publicly announced proposal to expand covered goods sectors to live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film equipment, or humanitarian cargo has been identified in the source content provided. *Research gap: whether downstream Commission review will expand covered goods to additional manufactured goods categories is not confirmed in available sources as of the source content date.*

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD (Tier 2).*

---

# Substantive Requirements

*Filtered to requirements operationally relevant to a freight forwarder on the Americas–Europe and Asia–Europe trade lanes.*

## Authorised Declarant Status

EU importers — or their indirect customs representatives — importing more than 50 tonnes of CBAM goods must hold authorised declarant status granted by the National Competent Authority (NCA) in the EU Member State where the importer is established. Access to the CBAM Registry is requested through the relevant NCA. A list of NCAs was published on 16 February 2026.

Where the workspace acts as indirect customs representative for clients who have not yet secured authorised declarant status, the workspace faces a practical decision: either advise the client to apply immediately, apply on the client's behalf (with legal clarity on liability), or decline to act as indirect customs representative for that transaction.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "CBAM — Do you import any of these products into the EU?" published 20 March 2026 and "List of National Competent Authorities for CBAM" published 16 February 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

## Embedded Emissions Declaration

Authorised declarants must declare the embedded greenhouse gas emissions (direct and, for cement and fertilisers, indirect emissions) in imported CBAM goods. Declarations are submitted annually. Default values and benchmarks for the definitive period have been published by the Commission (published 13 February 2026 as Excel files, per source content).

The workspace's role in this obligation is limited to: (a) ensuring that client importers are aware of the requirement, and (b) ensuring that import documentation captures the data elements that feed the annual declaration. The workspace does not itself make the emissions declaration unless it holds authorised declarant status.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "Default values and benchmarks definitive period," published 13 February 2026 (Tier 2).*

## Certificate Purchase and Surrender

CBAM certificates are purchased from national authorities by the authorised declarant. The price is set at the weekly average EU ETS auction price from 2027 (quarterly average for 2026). The first 2026 Q1 certificate price was published 7 April 2026. Surplus certificates (beyond 1/3 of total held) may be repurchased by national authorities.

This cost falls on the importer, not the workspace. However, where the workspace has assumed indirect customs representative status for covered goods imports, the commercial arrangement between the workspace and the client must address which party bears the certificate cost. **Legal Confirmation Required** on whether indirect customs representative status creates certificate purchase obligations for the workspace itself.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "First CBAM certificate price is now available," published 7 April 2026 (Tier 2).*

## CBAM Registry Integration with National Customs Systems

As of 1 January 2026, the CBAM Registry is integrated with national customs import systems, TARIC, and the EU Customs Single Window. This means customs declarations for covered goods must include valid CBAM account references in real time at the border. The workspace's customs filing systems must be capable of populating this field.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "CBAM successfully entered into force on 1 January 2026," published 14 January 2026. https://taxation-customs.ec.europa.eu/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism_en (Tier 2).*

---

# Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

The following events are sourced from announcements in the source content.

**1. CBAM methodology review — Call for Evidence (ongoing from 28 August 2025)**
- Issuing body: European Commission
- Subject: Emissions methodology rules, adjustment of CBAM certificates for EU ETS free allocation, and deduction rules for carbon price paid in third countries.
- Trigger: Commission launched this call for evidence on 28 August 2025.
- Impact on workspace: Outcomes could change the embedded emissions calculation methodology that declarant clients must apply. No outcome or closing date published in the source content provided. *Research gap: the closing date and Commission response timeline are not confirmed in the source content.*

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "Call for evidence on emission methodology," 28 August 2025 (Tier 2).*

**2. CBAM anti-circumvention and loophole-closing measures (Commission proposal, 17 December 2025)**
- Issuing body: European Commission
- Subject: Proposed measures to prevent circumvention of CBAM and strengthen its efficacy.
- Trigger: Commission proposal published 17 December 2025, described as a response to industry feedback.
- Impact on workspace: Could affect the scope of covered goods, enforcement mechanisms, or the definition of covered transactions. Legislative status of this proposal (Council and Parliament stages) is not confirmed in the source content. *Research gap: current legislative stage and expected enactment timeline are not confirmed in available source content.*

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "Commission strengthens the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism," 17 December 2025 (Tier 2).*

**3. CBAM certificate price publication cadence — transition to weekly averaging from 2027**
- Issuing body: European Commission
- Subject: Certificate price calculation shifts from quarterly average (2026) to weekly average from 2027.
- Impact on workspace: Clients managing CBAM certificate cost exposure will face more frequent price signals from 2027. The workspace can advise clients on this transition in forward planning conversations.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD (Tier 2).*

**4. Webinar series: "CBAM has launched: What changes for me?" — DG TAXUD (ongoing in 2026)**
- Issuing body: DG TAXUD
- First session: 19 March 2026. Additional sessions indicated in source content.
- Impact on workspace: Customs operations teams should access available recordings and materials (available on the EU Customs & Tax Learning Portal per source content) for staff training.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "CBAM webinar follow-up: What changes for me?" 26 March 2026 (Tier 2).*

---

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

## Authorised Declarant Registration

Registration is not a workspace obligation in its own right unless the workspace acts as indirect customs representative for CBAM-covered goods above the 50-tonne threshold. In that scenario, the workspace (or the importer it represents) must apply for authorised declarant status through the National Competent Authority in the relevant EU Member State.

Access to the CBAM Registry is requested through the NCA. The list of NCAs was published 16 February 2026.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD (Tier 2). Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

## Annual Declaration and Certificate Surrender

Authorised declarants must annually declare embedded emissions and surrender the corresponding number of CBAM certificates. This obligation rests on the declarant of record. Where the workspace holds declarant status, this obligation attaches. Where it does not, the obligation rests on the importer client.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

## Training Modules Available from Commission

As of 16 February 2026, the Commission published updated CBAM training modules (20 modules on the Authorisation Management Module and 3 new modules on Data Reconciliation for Monitoring and Control). These are available on the EU Customs & Tax Learning Portal. The workspace's customs brokerage teams should complete relevant modules.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "New CBAM trainings available," 16 February 2026 (Tier 2).*

## Note on Reporting Format Gaps

The source content confirms that implementing and delegated acts for the definitive regime have been adopted (referenced as the "December package"). The workspace should confirm that any customs management software used for EU import declarations has been updated to interface with the CBAM Registry's data requirements as specified in those acts.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD (Tier 2).*

---

# Operational System Requirements

| Requirement | Scope | Deadline | Gap from Current Operational Baseline |
|---|---|---|---|
| CBAM account number field in import documentation | All EU import declarations for shipments potentially containing covered goods | In force 1 January 2026 | If customs management systems do not include a CBAM account number / application reference number field, this is a systems gap requiring immediate remediation |
| Client portfolio audit for CBAM exposure | Freight forwarder's EU-bound client flows across Americas–Europe and Asia–Europe trade lanes | Immediate | The workspace must determine which clients import covered goods and whether the workspace holds indirect customs representative status for those transactions |
| Staff training on CBAM requirements | Customs brokerage and operations teams | Ongoing; new training modules published 16 February 2026 | Commission training modules are publicly available; gap is whether teams have completed them |
| Supplier/client communication protocol | Importers using the workspace as indirect customs representative | Immediate | Clients who have not yet obtained authorised declarant status or CBAM account numbers must be advised to do so; the workspace needs a documented escalation path |
| Legal review of indirect customs representation liability | Compliance and legal teams | Immediate | **Legal Confirmation Required**: liability scope for indirect customs representatives under CBAM has not been assessed in this brief and must be confirmed with qualified counsel in each Member State of operation |

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "Guidelines for Operational Procedures for 1 January 2026," published 19 March 2026 (Tier 2). Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

---

# Exemptions and Edge Cases

## Below-Threshold Exemption

Importers importing 50 tonnes or less of CBAM goods (single mass-based threshold) are not required to hold authorised declarant status. This threshold applies at the importer level, not the individual shipment level. Where the workspace processes small-lot shipments of steel or aluminium components incidental to its primary verticals (e.g., stage rigging hardware, automotive parts samples), it should assess whether the client importer's total annual imports of covered goods exceed the threshold across all channels.

*Source: EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD — "CBAM — Do you import any of these products into the EU?" published 20 March 2026 (Tier 2). Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

## Carbon Price Deduction

Where an importer can demonstrate that a carbon price was paid on embedded emissions during production in the country of origin, the corresponding amount may be deducted from the CBAM certificate surrender obligation. This is commercially relevant to clients in the workspace's portfolio importing goods from jurisdictions with established carbon pricing (e.g., certain production in China, which has an ETS, or the UK). The workspace can flag this deduction mechanism to clients, but the evidentiary requirements for the deduction are specified in implementing acts. **Legal Confirmation Required** on what documentation the workspace must collect or transmit to support a client's deduction claim.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1). EC CBAM Portal, DG TAXUD (Tier 2).*

## Workspace's Primary Cargo Verticals

Live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production assets, and humanitarian cargo are not covered goods under the current CBAM regime. CBAM does not currently apply to the workspace's primary cargo verticals. This remains the case unless the Commission expands the covered goods list.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/956, EUR-Lex (Tier 1).*

---

prior.intersection_summary: Overlaps with Regulation (EU) 2023/956 full text [EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R0956] as the binding legal source underlying all claims. Intersects with EU ETS Data Viewer [EEA] on certificate price linkage: CBAM certificate prices are derived from EU ETS allowance auction prices, creating a direct operational coupling between ETS price movements and CBAM certificate costs borne by importer clients. Intersects with Union Customs Code [Regulation (EU) No 952/2013] on the definition and liability framework for indirect customs representatives, which determines the workspace's compliance chain position.
